g.data-narration: Welcome to ARM Processor Exceptions and Modes. This topic covers critical concepts for embedded systems development.
g.text: Understanding Exception Handling in ARM Architecture

g.data-narration: An exception disrupts normal program flow, requiring special processor handling to respond to interrupts and faults.
g.text: An exception is an event that disrupts normal program execution and requires
the processor to take special action. When an exception occurs, the ARM
processor automatically switches to a specific mode to handle that exception.

g.data-narration: ARM processors are ubiquitous in embedded systems. Understanding exceptions is essential for writing robust interrupt-driven applications.
g.text: Mastering exceptions enables you to build
• Identify all seven ARM operation modes

g.data-narration: ARM provides seven operation modes: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, and System. Each serves a specific purpose in the system architecture.
Each mode is encoded with specific bits in CPSR: USR=0x10, FIQ=0x11, IRQ=0x12, SVC=0x13, ABT=0x17, UND=0x1B, SYS=0x1F.
User Mode is the normal program execution mode with limited privileges. Applications cannot access protected system resources directly.
IRQ mode handles standard interrupts with lower priority than FIQ. SVC mode is entered via SWI instructions or reset for OS kernel operations.
Abort mode handles memory faults. Undefined mode handles unimplemented instructions. System mode provides privileged access with user registers.
Exception vectors are fixed addresses where the processor jumps on specific exceptions. The table starts at 0x00000000 with each entry 4 bytes apart.
When an exception occurs, the processor automatically saves CPSR to SPSR, saves return address in LR, and jumps to the vector address.
g.text: • CPSR copied to SPSR of target mode

g.data-narration: ARM uses banked registers for efficient mode switching. Each mode has its own R13 (SP) and R14 (LR), with FIQ having dedicated R8-R12.
When multiple exceptions occur simultaneously, ARM handles them in priority order: Reset highest, then Data Abort, FIQ, IRQ, Prefetch Abort, Undefined, SWI lowest.
FIQ is faster with dedicated R8-R12 registers and higher priority. IRQ is for general interrupts. FIQ can preempt IRQ handling.
g.text: • More overhead due to register saving

g.data-narration: To return from exceptions, restore SPSR to CPSR and return to correct address. IRQ uses SUBS PC, LR, #4 to account for pipeline.
g.text: • Copy SPSR back to CPSR to restore processor state
• LR adjustment accounts for ARM pipeline

g.data-narration: The SWI instruction allows user programs to invoke supervisor calls. It switches to SVC mode for privileged operations like OS services.
g.text: SWI Example: User to Supervisor Transition

g.data-narration: Remember key formulas: vector address = base + exception_type × 4. Return address adjustment is #4 for IRQ mode.
In summary, ARM provides seven modes for different tasks, with a sophisticated exception handling mechanism for reliable embedded system operation.
g.text: ✓ Seven operation modes: User, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, Abort, Undefined, System
✓ Priority: Reset → Data Abort → FIQ → IRQ
✓ FIQ has dedicated R8-R12 for fastest handling
✓ Banked registers enable efficient mode switching
✓ Proper return from exceptions restores CPSR from SPSR
